screens.prompt: An ordinary PSD estimate over N frequency bins costs roughly one FFT's worth of work. Estimating the SCF over the same N frequency bins and, say, M candidate cycle frequencies — does the cost scale the same way, or does the extra dimension make it noticeably more expensive?
choices: Same order of cost — the α dimension is essentially free once you have the FFT
Noticeably more expensive — you're effectively repeating spectral-estimation work across many candidate α values
screens.reveal: Noticeably more expensive. Searching over M candidate cycle frequencies in addition to N frequency bins means the computation scales with both dimensions, not just one — real-time cyclic spectral estimation is a genuinely heavier computational load than an ordinary PSD, which is exactly why FAM/SSCA's efficient organization of the FFT work matters in practice, and why dedicated hardware (FPGA/ASIC) implementations of cyclic estimators are an active, legitimately useful engineering target rather than a solved problem.

screens.prompt: Why would an FPGA-accelerated implementation of FAM or SSCA be a meaningful engineering contribution rather than a solved, purely academic exercise?

choices.text: Real-time cyclic spectral estimation is computationally heavy (searching over both f and α), and efficient hardware mappings of FAM/SSCA are still an active area rather than a finished, off-the-shelf solution
FPGAs cannot run FFT-based algorithms at all
choices.feedback: FPGAs are routinely used to accelerate FFT-based signal processing — that's precisely why hardware-accelerated FAM/SSCA is a sensible target, not an impossible one.

choices.text: Cyclic spectral estimation has no real-time use cases
choices.feedback: Spectrum sensing, modulation classification, and non-cooperative signal detection (next module) are all naturally real-time or near-real-time tasks — the computational cost of cyclic estimation is exactly why efficient hardware matters for them.

screens.successFeedback: Right — this is the case's forward pointer to the research-frontiers module: the field's core theory is well established, but efficient real-time hardware implementation is still a genuinely open engineering problem, and one that plays directly to an FPGA/embedded background.
